data.title: JPMorgan now sees U.S. recession this year after Trump's tariffs
data.description:
JPMorgan on Friday updated its U.S. economic outlook and now sees the country slipping into a recession in 2025 after factoring in President Donald Trump's sweeping reciprocal tariffs. 

The imposition of the steepest American trade barriers in over a century has hammered sentiment across the globe. Wall Street slumped to its worst session since 2020 on Thursday, and the rout extended into Friday, bringing overall weekly losses to a whopping -9.08%.  

"We now expect real GDP to contract under the weight of the tariffs, and for the full year (4Q/4Q) we now look for real GDP growth of -0.3%, down from 1.3% previously. The recession in economic activity is projected to push the unemployment rate up to 5.3%," JPMorgan's Michael Feroli said, referring to gross domestic product.

See below the chart provided by JPMorgan with its updated forecasts:

"We expect the weakest months to be concentrated in the middle of the year. Even so, from a quarterly perspective we look for contractions in 3Q and 4Q," Feroli observed. 

JPMorgan also believes that the hit to consumers from tariff-sparked inflation could be higher than the jump in prices seen post the COVID-19 pandemic.

"The most readily quantifiable effect of higher tariffs on activity runs through higher inflation, and hence lower real income and lower real consumer spending. The pinch from higher prices that we expect in coming months may hit harder than in the post-pandemic inflation spike, as nominal income growth has been moderating recently, as opposed to accelerating in the earlier episode," Feroli said. 

"The forecasted contraction in economic activity is expected to depress hiring and over time to lift the unemployment rate to 5.3%. If realized, our stagflationary forecast would present a dilemma to Fed policymakers," he added. 

Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell on Friday said the economic impact of tariffs was likely to be larger than expected, adding that the central bank was in no hurry to act on interest rates and would wait and see how events play out.

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU). 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Trump administration declines expansion of coverage for costly obesity drugs
data.description:
The Trump administration held off on a highly anticipated decision regarding Medicare coverage for expensive obesity medications, according to a Bloomberg report.

According to the report, The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services had been reviewing a proposal from the Biden administration aimed at broadening access to these drugs for older Americans. 

Although the coverage expansion was not included in the latest regulatory update, CMS stated that it may revisit the proposal at a later date.

Shares of big weight-loss drugmakers like Eli Lilly & Co. fell 3.7%, Novo Nordisk A/S’s American depositary receipts down 1.7%.

data.title: Soybean futures sink after China slaps 34% tariff on U.S. goods; ADM, Bunge hit multiyear lows
data.description:
U.S. soybean futures plunged Friday after China levied a new 34% tariff on imported U.S. goods, of which soybeans are one of the largest, in response to President Trump's wave of "reciprocal" tariffs.

China already had imposed 10%-15% tariffs on ~$20B worth of agricultural imports from the U.S., including soybeans, effective since March 10, and suspended import qualifications for three U.S. entities, effectively halting their soybean shipments to China.

China already has been shifting its soybean purchases to alternative suppliers such as Brazil since the 2018 trade battle with the first Trump administration.

On the CBOT, soybeans (S_1:COM) for May delivery settled -3.4% to $9.86 1/2 per bushel and wheat (W_1:COM) for May delivery finished -1.1% to $5.30 1/4 per bushel, but corn (C_1:COM) for May delivery closed +0.7% at $4.60 per bushel.

ETFs: (SOYB), (WEAT), (CORN), (DBA), (MOO)

Corn eventually reversed course after grains had plummeted early in the session, with traders evaluating how much China's retaliatory tariffs on U.S. exports will really hurt or for how long the. tariffs will be in place.

China "cannot sustain a war," Daniel Flynn of Price Futures Group said, "meaning [that] they need our market more than we need theirs."

According to USDA data, China purchased more than 22M metric tons of U.S. soybeans in the last marketing year, compared with sales of just under 3M metric tons for corn.

Shares of agriculture commodity trading giants Archer Daniels Midland (NYSE:ADM) and Bunge (BG) closed -8.9% and -6.4%, respectively, while hitting their lowest levels since August 2020 and February 2021, on worries that falling soybean prices - partially due to the trade conflict with China - could hurt their already weak earnings.

ADM (NYSE:ADM) and Bunge (BG) already have suffered sharp declines since 2022 due to their weak financial performance, caused partially by declining soybean prices as favorable weather in major producing countries led to increased supplies; CBOT soybean prices have tumbled from ~$15 per bushel two years ago to below $10 this week.

Still, soybean prices will find a new balance if the tariffs stay, Morningstar strategist Seth Goldstein told Barron's: "If we're looking at the very near term, something like tariffs can have an impact. But longer term, if China sticks with the U.S. soybean tariff, we'd likely see American soybeans go to other destinations. Big firms like ADM and Bunge are buying and selling crops from all over the world to all over the world. I think it would be easier for them to forge new trade paths."

data.title: Cogent announces IPv4 address securitization offering
data.description:
• Cogent Communications Holdings (NASDAQ:CCOI) on Friday announced that a special-purpose, bankruptcy remote, indirect wholly owned subsidiary of the company has priced $174.4M aggregate principal amount of secured Internet Protocol version 4 ("IPv4") address revenue term notes, 6.646% Series 2025-1.
  • The notes will have an anticipated repayment term of five years.
  • Cogent intends to use the net proceeds of the offering for general corporate purposes.
  • Cogent expects the notes transaction to close on or around April 11, 2025.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Match says activist claims 'inaccurate and misleading'
data.description:
Match Group (NASDAQ:MTCH) on Friday said activist investor Anson Funds Management made “inaccurate and misleading characterizations” about the online dating service provider. 

Anson Funds Portfolio Manager Sagar Gupta earlier in the day said in a letter that it “has no choice but to publicly pursue refreshment through replacement. 

Anson said its private feedback to Match was “met with great resistance.” He called Match’s current board as “outdated, insular,” adding that it is “inadequately qualified to oversee a modern technology company primarily serving young adults.”

A Match spokesperson told Seeking Alpha that Anson’s letter “contains inaccurate and misleading characterizations of the company and our engagement with the investor.”

According to the spokesperson, Match’s board has “deep expertise and diverse perspectives across technology, consumer and financial sectors.”

Anson said it was nominating three candidates to Match’s board at the company’s annual shareholders meeting. They include Fumbi Chima, Laura Lee, and Kelley Morrell. Chima has been a technology executive at Adidas (OTCQX:ADDDF)(OTCQX:ADDYY), Fox Networks, and Walmart (WMT), while Lee has held content leadership positions with YoutTube and Twitch. Morrell was a board member of Bumble (BMBL), a Match rival. 

Match’s spokesperson said the board has reviewed the nominees in accordance with its governance policies. 

Anson said Match’s board “only considered” changes after “extreme shareholder pressure,” criticizing the panel for a series of CEO changes instead of resetting its ranks. In February, Zillow Group (Z)(ZG) co-founder Spencer Rascoff joined Match’s board last year after talks with Elliott Investment Management. 

Rascoff, “in partnership with the management team and the board, is actively addressing key strategic priorities to drive sustainable growth and create long-term stockholder value,” the Match’s spokesperson said.
